items.title: HY Spreads at 2.85% — The Dangerous Calm Before Credit Reprices
items.idea: High yield OAS sits at 2.85% (Apr 15, 2026), near multi-cycle lows. VIX is at 18.17 after a March spike; 10y-2y spread at 0.53%. Gayed's core thesis: credit doesn't reprice gradually — it reprices through liquidity. When the marginal buyer steps away, spreads can gap far more than fundamentals suggest because the market cannot absorb one-way flows. The most dangerous moments in credit are when spreads are tight, liquidity feels abundant, and investors assume the risk is already behind them.
items.angle: With PCE at 3.8% and Fed minutes signaling hike openness, tight credit faces a double risk: spreads that don't compensate AND a macro catalyst building above. The asymmetry here is brutal — carry is thin, downside is fat.
items.tweetA: HY spreads at 2.85%. Not a warning for later. A warning for now — while nobody is worried. Credit doesn't ring a bell at the top. It just stops compensating you for risk. That is exactly where we are.
items.tweetB: The dangerous credit setup isn't wide spreads. It's tight spreads plus confident vol suppression. HY OAS 2.85%, VIX 18, curve mildly positive. Every box checked for all-clear. Every box checked for late cycle. Same thing.

items.title: The Utilities Signal: Institutional De-Risking Before Spreads Move
items.idea: Gayed's JOJO ETF thesis: Utilities relative performance vs. S&P 500 historically acts as a leading signal for rising equity volatility, which in turn leads credit spread expansion. When institutions prefer stability and dividend-like cash flows, Utilities outperform — and because equity vol and credit spreads are well-correlated, that defensive turn can signal HY risk before spreads actually widen. JOJO rotates between HY and long-duration Treasuries based on this signal.
items.angle: If Utilities are beginning to lead in a market that just posted nine green weeks, it's worth asking whether this is the quiet rotation that precedes the repricing. The signal isn't screaming yet — that's the point.
items.tweetA: Nobody watches Utilities as a credit signal. That's exactly why it works. Institutional de-risking shows up in defensive equity rotation before it shows up in spreads. The early warning is in an asset class most people ignore.
items.tweetB: Nine green S&P weeks, yet Utilities as a sector have held bid. Either institutions are wrong and early, or they're seeing something the ATH tape isn't showing. The divergence between offensive equity records and defensive equity strength is the thing worth monitoring.

items.title: 70% Probability of Fed HIKE Priced — S&P 500 at All-Time Highs. Something Is Inverted.
items.idea: Fed funds futures are pricing roughly a 70% probability of a rate HIKE by year-end. Kevin Warsh, recently sworn in as Fed Chair, has telegraphed willingness to defend credibility against CPI at 3.8%. Simultaneously, the S&P 500 posted fresh all-time highs for an eighth consecutive week and the Magnificent Seven contributed 85% of the S&P's May advance. Textbook duration math says a rate-hike environment crushes long-duration assets — yet the longest-duration corner of the market is leading.
items.angle: The duration playbook is not merely wrong — it is inverted. Either AI earnings have permanently broken the relationship between discount rates and growth multiples, or the market is narrowing into the last clean story while macro distribution widens.
items.tweetA: Fed funds futures: 70% probability of a HIKE. S&P 500: all-time high. The thing textbook says should be most under pressure from rising rates is leading the tape. Either earnings have permanently broken duration math, or this is the last chapter before reversion.
items.tweetB: In 1994, 2018, and 2022 a hawkish pivot crushed long-duration assets hard. This time a hike is being PRICED IN while Mag7 runs to records. I do not know which tail is right. But I know the market is assuming only one of them exists.

charts.desc: Charts referenced: S&P 500 ATH alongside fed funds futures pricing hike probability

items.title: Mag7 = 85% of S&P May Gains — Flight to Quality or Flight to Narrative?
items.idea: The Magnificent Seven contributed 85% of the S&P 500's May advance during a month when a rate hike was being priced. NVIDIA alone reported $81.6B quarterly revenue (+85% YoY) and Jensen Huang placed total AI addressable market at $50-80 trillion. The market's internal narrative: earnings are so dominant that duration sensitivity no longer applies. Gayed's counter: this is not flight to quality — it is flight to narrative. The market is narrowing into the smallest possible cohort that can still tell a clean story while macro distribution widens.
items.angle: NVIDIA earnings at $81.6B are real. The $50-80T TAM may be real. But the Nifty Fifty earnings in 1972 were real too. What breaks is not the earnings — it is the multiple. When every incremental dollar of risk appetite flows into 6 names, the exit problem is structural.
items.tweetA: NVIDIA: $81.6B quarter, 85% YoY growth, Jensen Huang says $50-80 trillion TAM. The earnings are real. But Mag7 delivering 85% of index gains while a HIKE is priced — is that flight to quality, or is it the market hiding in the only story left that still makes sense?
items.tweetB: The Nifty Fifty parallel is not about valuation. It is about concentration. When macro uncertainty is high, capital narrows into the names with the cleanest earnings story. The exit becomes a problem the day the story needs updating.

items.title: Small Caps Also Rallying Despite Rate-Hike Environment — Playbook Has No Safe Shorts Left
items.idea: The Russell 2000 has joined the rally despite being the cohort that textbook says should be most damaged in a rate-hike environment: floating-rate debt, thinner balance sheets, higher refinancing costs. Both small caps AND the longest-duration large caps are rallying simultaneously as a hike gets priced. When every segment of the equity market is bid despite divergent macro sensitivity, either the economy is far stronger than the data shows, or the last shorts have already been squeezed out.
items.angle: When the rate-hike playbook has no safe short — because even the most rate-sensitive assets are rallying — it usually means positioning has become the dominant factor over fundamentals. That is not a stable equilibrium.
items.tweetA: Russell 2000 rallying while a Fed rate HIKE is being priced. Floating-rate debt. Thin balance sheets. Higher refinancing costs. Should hate this environment more than anyone. Yet it is joining the run. When the playbook has no safe short, it usually means the last bear has covered.
items.tweetB: Both Mag7 and Russell 2000 are up while a hike is priced. They have opposite exposures to higher rates. Both cannot be right simultaneously. One of them is being carried by flow rather than fundamental logic.

items.title: Term Premium at 0.83%; Kevin Warsh Takes Fed Gavel in June With Inflation Accelerating
items.idea: The 10-year term premium (SF Fed estimate) sits at 0.83% — meaningfully positive and rising. Investors are demanding compensation for holding long-dated paper given CPI re-acceleration, energy +17.9% YoY, and a new Fed Chair telegraphing hawkishness. Kevin Warsh inherits the Fed at the June meeting with PCE at 3.8%, markets at all-time highs, and a 70% probability of a hike already in futures. In every prior historical parallel (Greenspan 1994, Powell 2018, Powell 2022), this macro setup crushed high-multiple growth hard.
items.angle: The June FOMC is the event risk that the market has not priced. Warsh walking in with a mandate to defend credibility, rising term premium, and sticky inflation — the first communication from the new chair will reprice the long end. The 10-year at 4.45% is not the ceiling.
items.tweetA: Kevin Warsh takes the Fed gavel in June with: CPI 3.8%, energy +17.9% YoY, 10-year term premium at 0.83%, and markets at all-time highs. He has one job — defend the Fed's credibility. The June meeting is not currently priced for what that job requires.
items.tweetB: Term premium at 0.83% is the bond market charging an uncertainty surcharge. Greenspan 1994, Powell 2018, Powell 2022 — each time this setup crushed long-duration assets. Markets are betting this time is different. History is not repeating. But it may be rhyming on a delay.

items.title: Nine Consecutive S&P Weekly Gains — 11th Time in History, Twice in 20 Years
items.idea: The S&P 500 closed its ninth consecutive weekly gain — occurring only eleven times since reliable weekly index data began, and only twice in the past two decades. Dow 50,000, Nikkei 65,000, KOSPI 8,000 all printed as milestones simultaneously. The streak signals not just strength but an unusual absence of sellers. Nine weeks without one setback means every investor who wanted to sell has already sold. The absence of selling pressure itself carries information about what comes next.
items.angle: When sellers are fully exhausted and new highs attract no profit-taking, the next move depends entirely on what happens to the marginal buyer. AI spending data and Hormuz ceasefire were the two catalysts. If either fades, there is no natural floor built from accumulated seller capitulation.
items.tweetA: Nine consecutive S&P weekly gains. 11th time in history. Twice in 20 years. The message is not just strength — it is that every seller who wanted to sell has already sold. What comes next when there are no natural sellers left depends entirely on whether the marginal buyer stays.
items.tweetB: Dow 50,000. Nikkei 65,000. KOSPI 8,000. Nine green S&P weeks. And April PCE just printed 3.8%. Markets and macro are not in agreement. One of them will need to resolve into the other. The divergence is the widest it has been since early 2022.

charts.desc: Chart: S&P 500 nine consecutive weekly gains marked against historical instances — worth opening

items.title: April PCE 3.8% / Core 3.3% — Fed Minutes Signal Rate Hike Openness for First Time
items.idea: April headline PCE rose 3.8% YoY (core 3.3%) — hottest since May 2023. Energy did the lifting (+17.9% YoY), which the Fed historically tries to look through. But the Fed's April meeting minutes showed a majority of participants signaling openness to rate hikes if inflation remains persistent. The committee has held at 3.50-3.75% for three consecutive meetings. Kevin Warsh inherits this at the June FOMC — with inflation accelerating, not decelerating.
items.angle: The Hormuz ceasefire could quickly unwind the energy inflation driver — but that depends on a 30-day geopolitical timeline that is not guaranteed. The Fed cannot price in a ceasefire. It can only respond to data. PCE at 3.8% and mounting hawkishness are the data.
items.tweetA: April PCE 3.8%, core 3.3% — hottest since May 2023. Fed minutes: majority open to rate hikes. Kevin Warsh takes the gavel in June. The market just posted nine green weeks pricing the opposite scenario. Something is going to have to reconcile.
items.tweetB: The Fed has held at 3.50-3.75% for three consecutive meetings while inflation re-accelerated. Now the minutes admit hikes are back on the table. If the Hormuz ceasefire falls through and energy stays elevated, the June meeting becomes a live event nobody is positioned for.

charts.desc: Chart: Headline and core PCE inflation year-over-year — worth opening

items.title: HY at 2.72% vs. 5.18% Long-Term Average — Consumer Real Spending +0.1%, Savings Near 4-Year Low
items.idea: HY credit spreads closed May 28 at 2.72% against a long-term average of 5.18% — one of the tightest readings of the current cycle. Simultaneously, real PCE grew only 0.1% in April and the personal saving rate sits near a four-year low. Credit markets are priced for a soft landing. The consumer data — thin spending growth and a depleted savings buffer — suggests the consumer is bending exactly when risk assets are pricing perfection.
items.angle: 2.72% vs. 5.18% long-term average: you're giving back 2.46% of carry compensation relative to history while the data that would justify that compression is deteriorating. The asymmetry is particularly brutal here.
items.tweetA: HY spreads 2.72% vs. 5.18% long-term average. Real consumer spending +0.1% in April. Savings rate near a 4-year low. Credit is priced for a soft landing. The consumer data says the landing is already bumpy. Both cannot be right.
items.tweetB: Either the consumer recovers and HY at 2.72% looks cheap, or the consumer cracks and HY gaps from historically tight levels. There is no gradual repricing in high yield. The payoff on being wrong here is asymmetric, and not in your favor.

items.title: US-Iran Hormuz Ceasefire: Tehran to Remove Mines in 30 Days — Brent Down 7% on the Week
items.idea: Reports from negotiating channels indicate Tehran would remove mines from the Strait of Hormuz within 30 days in exchange for gradual lifting of the US naval blockade and partial sanctions relief. About 20% of global oil and LNG transits through Hormuz. The possibility of normalization sent Brent crude down nearly 7% — its sharpest weekly decline in two months. The energy price drop is also the primary driver of the week's PCE headline reading. The ceasefire is a 30-day geopolitical bet, not a resolved outcome.
items.angle: The market has priced the good Hormuz outcome. If the 30-day timeline slips or negotiations break down, energy reverses, PCE stays elevated, and the Fed's hand is forced. The Hormuz bet and the rate-cut bet are structurally the same trade — both require geopolitical cooperation.
items.tweetA: Iran reportedly agreeing to remove mines from the Strait of Hormuz. Brent -7% in a week. 20% of global oil and LNG transits that chokepoint. The market priced the good outcome in 5 sessions. If the 30-day timeline slips, energy reverses and the PCE inflation driver is back. $VLO $MPC $XLE
items.tweetB: The Hormuz ceasefire trade and the AI growth trade are running simultaneously. One requires geopolitical goodwill to hold for 30+ days, the other requires earnings execution to continue. The market is betting both happen. Binary risks rarely both resolve at the optimistic end.

charts.desc: Chart: Brent and WTI crude oil prices, week ending May 29, 2026 — worth opening

items.title: Dell AI Server Revenue 7x YoY to $16.1B — Backlog $24.4B, Full-Year Outlook $167B
items.idea: Dell reported quarterly revenue of $43.8B (+88% YoY), with AI server revenue alone growing more than sevenfold to $16.1B. Management raised the AI server backlog to $24.4B and lifted the full-year revenue outlook to $167B. This is shipped product and a committed backlog — not a forecast about future demand. The AI infrastructure buildout has moved from abstract to concrete.
items.angle: A $24.4B backlog means at least two more quarters of AI server delivery are already contracted. Bears who argued AI capex was decelerating cited the wrong data set. The question is not whether the buildout is real — it clearly is — but what comes after the delivery cycle completes.
items.tweetA: Dell AI server revenue grew 7x in one year to $16.1B. Backlog: $24.4B. Full-year outlook: $167B. This is not speculative demand — this is shipped product and committed orders. Every model that said AI capex was peaking needs to absorb this print.
items.tweetB: At $167B full-year revenue guidance, Dell is now larger than Coca-Cola and approaching McDonald's in annual revenue — growing at 88% YoY. The AI infrastructure buildout is not a 2024 or 2025 story. It is a multi-year delivery cycle that is still accelerating.

items.title: HALO Framework: AI Capex Cycle Rotating Toward Heavy Assets — Mines, Railroads, Power Utilities
items.idea: Matt Tuttle's HALO (Heavy Assets, Low Obsolescence) lens argues the AI conversation has fixated on 6 hyperscaler names for two years while missing the next rotation: physical infrastructure AI cannot exist without and that cannot be disrupted by the next model release. Mines, railroads, energy companies, and power utilities — assets with physical moats. The claim: AI capex phases from semiconductors → data centers → power and physical infrastructure, and we are somewhere between Phase 2 and 3.
items.angle: Copper, uranium, and grid infrastructure have been pricing this rotation for 18 months. The equity community is just starting to label it. When the McKinsey high-growth industries chart and the HALO thesis converge, the institutional allocation is not far behind.
items.tweetA: The AI trade has obsessed over 6 names for 2 years. Matt Tuttle's HALO thesis: what happens when compute is no longer the bottleneck? Power grids, mine infrastructure, railroads, pipelines — physical assets AI cannot exist without and cannot disrupt. Phase 3 of the capex cycle.
items.tweetB: AI capex: Phase 1 was semiconductors. Phase 2 was data centers. Phase 3 is power and physical infrastructure. We are somewhere between Phase 2 and 3. The commodity complex has been pricing this. $VALE $RIO $NEE $SO $UNP are the HALO names to know.

items.title: AUKUS Deploys Autonomous Undersea Drones to Guard Cables and Pipelines — 2027 Target
items.idea: The US, UK, and Australia are expanding AUKUS beyond nuclear submarines into autonomous undersea systems, targeting 2027 operational readiness for protecting subsea cables, pipelines, and naval approaches. The bottleneck was never firepower — it was persistence. Manned submarines are too scarce and expensive for continuous cable corridor patrol. Autonomous vehicles transform the problem from 'deploy a capital asset' to 'seed the battlespace with expendable sensors.' Radio dies underwater, forcing higher autonomy at the edge.
items.angle: The long-term industrial money is not the drone hull — it is the maintenance contract, sensor stack, autonomy software, secure comms, and data pipeline. This is the razor/blade model applied to undersea defense. Defense contractors building the data stack own the recurring revenue.
items.tweetA: AUKUS is building an undersea drone network to guard cables and pipelines — 2027 target. The bottleneck was never firepower. It was persistence. You cannot send a nuclear submarine to patrol every cable junction. Autonomous vehicles change that equation permanently.
items.tweetB: The long-term money in undersea defense is not the drone hull. It is the maintenance contract, sensor stack, autonomy software, and data pipeline. The drone is the razor. The data is the blade. Every successful defense platform since GPS has monetized the same way.

items.title: 17 Nations Sign Subsea Cable Pact at Shangri-La — US and China Both Absent
items.idea: At the Shangri-La Dialogue, 17 nations launched principles for protecting underwater infrastructure. Both the United States and China were absent. McDonough frames this as the fundamental split: norms are being written in one room; operational capability is being built in another. Australia's defense minister has stated there is 'no plan B' if the nuclear submarine element fails — making the drone program a necessary faster deliverable. AUKUS Pillar II effectively locks UK and Australian industrial capacity into US-led command architecture.
items.angle: The 17-nation pact without US or China is diplomatic theater. The real line of scrimmage is AUKUS Pillar II vs. whatever China is deploying in the South China Sea. Principles with no enforcement mechanism don't deter adversaries who have operational capability.
items.tweetA: 17 nations signed a subsea cable protection pact at Shangri-La. US and China were both absent. The author nails it: norms are being written in one room; operational capability is being built in another. This is how every major geopolitical domain has developed since 2015.
items.tweetB: The seabed favors ambiguity. A cut cable can be blamed on anchors or fishing gear — that attribution lag is exactly why autonomous drones collecting pattern-of-life data before incidents matter. Russia understood this in the Baltic. AUKUS is building the counter.

items.title: Silver: Structural Demand Meets Stagnant Supply — 'Not a Speculative Bubble'
items.idea: Tavi Costa analyzes silver prices vs. global production, arguing this is a structural market adjustment, not a speculative bubble. Mine output cannot scale quickly: discoveries are rarer, permitting is harder, ore grades are falling, and development timelines keep extending. The commodity cycle model — 'high prices cure high prices through new supply' — has broken down for silver. Costa's kicker: 'Wake me up when production actually starts surging.'
items.angle: Copper got the AI/grid narrative first. Silver has the additional monetary bid and is also critical to the energy transition and AI power buildout. When supply cannot respond to price signals, the commodity bull market persists until demand destruction — which is not happening yet.
items.tweetA: Silver mine supply is not responding to price. Rarer discoveries, harder permitting, falling ore grades, longer development timelines. The high-prices-cure-high-prices model has broken down. That is what structural means in commodity markets. Wake me up when production actually surges.
items.tweetB: Copper got the AI narrative. Silver is waiting for it. Both are critical to energy transition and AI power buildout. Silver also has the monetary bid. And supply cannot grow fast. This is the setup before the consensus finds it — which is the only time it is actionable.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING — Silver prices vs. global production: structural demand vs. stagnant supply curve divergence. Charts inside.

items.title: Zinc: Emerging as Most Compelling Opportunity Across the Commodity Complex
items.idea: Tavi Costa flags zinc as one of the most compelling opportunities across the commodity complex. The same structural dynamics apply as silver: supply underinvestment, longer development timelines, constrained new capacity. Unlike silver, zinc lacks the monetary narrative — which means less crowded positioning. Costa frames this as the setup before the macro community labels the trade.
items.angle: Zinc vs. silver: one has the narrative (monetary metal, energy transition), the other doesn't yet. The best commodity setups in history have been the industrial metals without a consensus narrative — positioned before the macro community labels the trade.
items.tweetA: Tavi Costa puts zinc in the same structural supply-constraint category as silver. Base metal with genuine industrial demand and underinvestment in new capacity. No narrative yet. No crowded positioning yet. That is typically when the setup is cleanest.
items.tweetB: Zinc has no AI story, no energy transition story, no monetary metal story. It is just: supply cannot grow fast enough to meet industrial demand. The best commodity setups are the ones the narrative has not found yet. $ZINC

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening alongside the silver analysis

items.title: Gamma Squeeze Probability Increasing in $PURR
items.idea: Capital Flows flagged in a subscriber chat thread that the gamma squeeze probability in $PURR is increasing. A gamma squeeze occurs when options dealers, short gamma, are forced to buy the underlying to hedge as price rises — creating self-reinforcing upward pressure. The setup is mechanical, not fundamental: when dealer gamma exposure tips sufficiently short, even modest buying pressure can accelerate into a sharp move.
items.angle: Gamma squeezes are not a thesis — they are a mechanical event with a timer. The fundamental question is not whether $PURR is 'worth it' but whether the gamma structure makes a move inevitable. Position sizing logic: small, defined risk, short duration.
items.tweetA: Capital Flows flagging increasing gamma squeeze probability in $PURR. Gamma squeezes are not an investment thesis — they are a mechanical event. When options dealers are forced to hedge by buying the underlying, the price move becomes self-reinforcing regardless of fundamentals.
items.tweetB: The $PURR gamma setup: when dealer gamma exposure goes sufficiently short, even moderate buying pressure cascades. The math is the thesis. The question is not valuation — it is whether the structure tips before or after you enter. $PURR

items.title: McKinsey Featured Chart: The High-Growth Fields Transforming the Economy
items.idea: McKinsey's featured chart this week maps the high-growth industries currently transforming the economy. No article text in email — chart-only format. Based on the framing, this is likely a sector-level growth rate analysis comparing AI, energy transition, defense, and other high-velocity industries against historical benchmarks. These McKinsey charts often define the institutional language that large-cap allocators use to frame sector rotation in the following 6-12 months.
items.angle: McKinsey charting a sector signals it has moved from early majority to institutional adoption framing. The industries they identify as 'high-growth' tend to receive the next wave of large-cap fund inflows. Worth understanding the specific rankings.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's featured chart this week: the high-growth fields transforming the economy. When McKinsey publishes a sector growth chart, the institutional allocation discussion is already happening. The chart is descriptive. The implication is prescriptive for where passive flows go next.
items.tweetB: The fastest-growing sectors of any decade define where capital flows the following decade. McKinsey mapped this week's edition. The question to ask: which are structural (AI, energy transition, defense) versus cyclical? Charts inside — worth opening.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING — McKinsey featured chart: high-growth fields transforming the economy. Charts inside at the McKinsey link.

items.title: McKinsey: Stablecoins Find Their Niche
items.idea: McKinsey published a chart titled 'Stablecoins find their niche' as part of this week's data series. No article text available. The McKinsey framing — 'niche' rather than 'disruption' — suggests the analysis maps where stablecoins have actually achieved adoption: payment rails, treasury management, cross-border settlement. When McKinsey charts stablecoin adoption, the regulatory and banking conversation has already moved past 'if' to 'how.'
items.angle: The stablecoin regulatory framework is clarifying in the US and EU simultaneously. McKinsey charting the niche is the signal that banks and payment processors have begun modeling stablecoins as infrastructure rather than competition. $USDC $PYUSD and their banking partners are the trade.
items.tweetA: McKinsey says stablecoins have found their niche. Not DeFi hype — payment rails, treasury management, cross-border settlement. When McKinsey publishes a chart on stablecoin adoption, the banking and regulatory conversation has moved past 'if' to 'how fast.' $USDC
items.tweetB: Stablecoin 'finding a niche' in McKinsey language means: the use case is proven, the regulatory pathway is clarifying, and institutional adoption is beginning. This is the same language they used for cloud computing in 2012 and mobile payments in 2015. Charts inside.

charts.desc: Charts inside — McKinsey stablecoin niche analysis, worth opening

items.title: McKinsey: The Aerospace Supplier Split
items.idea: McKinsey's third chart this week covers 'the aerospace supplier split' — dividing the industry into winners and losers. No article text in email. The likely framing: post-COVID supply chain stress has permanently bifurcated aerospace suppliers into those who retooled for defense + commercial recovery versus those who didn't. Defense demand (drones, missiles, autonomous systems) is fundamentally different from commercial aviation demand (narrowbody, MRO). Companies positioned for both are scarce.
items.angle: The aerospace supply chain constraint has not fully resolved — the bottleneck moved from engine production to narrowbody components. The AUKUS underwater drone program adds another autonomous systems demand layer. Suppliers with exposure to both are the rare dual-benefit names.
items.tweetA: McKinsey charts the aerospace supplier split this week. Defense demand and commercial aviation demand are not the same business. Post-COVID, the supply chain bifurcated permanently between those who retooled and those who didn't. The companies positioned for both are scarce. Charts inside.
items.tweetB: Aerospace supply chain stress is still not resolved — the bottleneck just moved. AUKUS undersea drones add another autonomous systems demand layer on top of existing defense orders. The suppliers who can serve defense autonomy AND commercial aviation recovery are the rarest names in the sector.

charts.desc: Charts inside — McKinsey aerospace supplier split analysis, worth opening
